title: Mutton Pie
subtitle: Slow-braised mutton shoulder under a rough shortcrust lid, rich with onion and herbs
excerpt: Mutton is older, fattier, and more flavourful than lamb. Braised shoulder set in a deep dish under a shortcrust pastry lid produces a pie with a depth of flavour that lamb cannot match. It is a cold-weather pie, best made a day ahead.
sourceNotes: Traditional British and Scottish. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: mutton pie method. F. Marian McNeill, The Scots Kitchen (1929): mutton and vegetable pie. Traditional British and Welsh farmhouse cooking.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unbaked pie for up to 2 months. Bake from frozen at 190°C for 55-60 minutes, covering loosely with foil if the pastry colours too quickly.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Braise the mutton a day ahead. The flavour improves overnight. Assemble and bake on the day.
recipe.temperatureNote: fan oven for both braising and baking the pastry lid

glossaryTerms.definition: Meat from a sheep over two years old. It has a deeper, more complex flavour than lamb and a higher fat content. In the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries mutton was more prized than lamb in Britain; today it is available from specialist butchers and halal meat shops.
A pie baked in a deep dish with only a pastry lid, not a pastry lining on the base or sides. The filling sits directly in the dish; the lid is pressed onto the rim. Also called a single-crust pie.

content.text: needs long, slow cooking to become tender. The shoulder, cut into large chunks, benefits from 2 hours of braising in stock and aromatics. The reward is a sauce that is deeply savoury, and meat that is yielding and rich in a way that lamb shoulder rarely achieves.
: the filling goes into the dish and the pastry sits on top, pressed onto the rim. It is easier to execute than a fully lined pie and the base requires no separate cooking.

items.prepNote: shoulder, cut into large chunks, or use lamb shoulder
for dusting and to thicken the sauce

content.text: in a casserole over high heat. Dust the
chunks with flour and season. Brown in batches; remove. Soften the onion and carrot for 5 minutes. Add garlic, rosemary, bay. Return the meat. Add
if using and reduce by half. Add
. Cover and cook in a 160°C oven for 2 hours until the meat is tender and the sauce is thick. Season with
Heat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan). Spoon the cold filling into a deep pie dish. Roll the
to 4 mm thickness and lay it over the dish. Press to seal, trim, and crimp. Score the surface and cut a steam vent. Brush with beaten egg. Bake for 35-40 minutes until deeply golden.

rows.symptom: Mutton is still tough after 2 hours
rows.fix: Return to the oven for 30-minute increments. Mutton can take up to 3 hours in some cases.

rows.cause: Insufficient flour for thickening, or lid was left off
rows.fix: After braising, reduce the sauce by simmering uncovered for 10 minutes before the pie is assembled.
Aim for a consistent 4 mm; thinner pastry tears when draped over a full dish.

rows.cause: Mutton was not browned adequately at the start
rows.fix: Brown the meat in properly hot oil, in batches, until deeply coloured. The fond from browning is the foundation of the flavour.

content.text: Mutton pie sits in the same tradition as the British steak and kidney pie: a savoury, substantial winter dish that makes the most of a flavourful cut of meat. It is a dish that rewards patience and advance planning, and one that improves overnight in the refrigerator before the pastry lid goes on.
